Data-Story-3 Walker Briggs 2026-04-09 Chapter 1 Sustainable Development Goals The Sustainable Development Goals provide an action driven framework to help tackle some of the most pressing issues in society today. In total, there are seventeen goals that range from local issues to global issues. For Data Story 3 and for the final project, Sustainable Development Goals number 4 and 5 will be of focus. SDG 4 represents quality education and SDG 5 represents gender equality. These two Sustainable Development Goals go hand-in-hand with each other and analyzing them separately would be counterproductive. The United Nations Conference on Sustainable Development in Rio de Janeiro in 2012 was a critical moment in the creation of the Sustainable Development Goals (UNDP, n.d.). The objective of these seventeen goals was to address current environmental, political, and economic issues. Before the Sustainable Development Goals were established, there were Millennium Development Goals (MDGs) which began in the early 2000s. The MDGs were in place to target “indignity of poverty) (UNDP, n.d.). The SDGs are a more updated version of the MDGs, creating a more aggressive approach to environmental sustainability.

Chapter 2 SDG 4: Quality Education SDG 4 is one of if not the most important Sustainable Development Goal. The goal of SDG 4 is to “ensure inclusive, equitable, and quality education for all while promoting lifelong learning opportunities” (Gongade et al. 2025). Not only is quality education incredibly important for children with developing brains, but it is also the foundation of breaking the poverty cycle. Quality education can boost an entire nation’s GDP while at the same time improving health and wellness conditions for all. Progress has been made in providing quality education, especially in developing countries yet more needs to be done. Since 2015, 109 million additional children are now enrolled in primary and secondary school (The Global Goals, n.d.). Though this statistic proves that progress has been made, a study conducted by the United Nations in 2023 found that around 272 million children were still not enrolled in primary or secondary school (The Global Goals, n.d.). Least developed countries experience the worst effects due to the lack of resources towards receiving and maintaining a quality education. One of the key drives of achieving SDG 4 is the training and utilization of quality teachers (Ydo 2022). Within SDG 4, the United Nations has a multi targeted approach to quality education. The specific targets for SDG 4 and quality education are: (4.1) Free primary and secondary education, (4.2) Equal access to quality pre-primary education, (4.3) Equal access to affordable technical, vocational and higher education, (4.4) Increase the number of people with relevant skills for financial success, (4.5) Eliminate all discrimination in education, (4.6) Universal literacy and numeracy, (4.7) Education for sustainable development and global citizenship, (4.8) Build and upgrade inclusive and safe schools, (4.9) Expand higher education scholarships for developing countries, (4A) Increase the supply of quality teachers in developing countries (The Global Goals, n.d.). There are many fascinating statistics that will help to provide data driven context. From 2015 to 2024, primary school completion rate increased from 85% to 88%. Lower secondary completion rate increased from 74% to 78% from 2015 to 2024. Also from 2015 to 2024, upper secondary completion rate increased from 53% to 60% (The Global Goals, n.d.). In 2019, 58% of children have achieved minimum reading proficiency globally and 44% reached the minimum mathematics proficiency (The Global Goals, n.d.). Though global education completion rates were increasing in 2019, they began to decrease from this point on with nearly a 15 point decline in mathematics proficiency and a 10 point decline in reading proficiency. This may be attributed to Covid-19 and the ripple effects that were sent throughout each nation and education system. Only 2 in 3 children in Sub-Saharan Africa complete primary school on-time and globally, only half of primary schools have the basic infrastructure to support children with disabilities. With this in mind, around 40% of countries have not reached gender parity in primary school education.

Chapter 3 SDG 5: Gender Equality Sustainable Development Goal 5 is deeply interwoven with SDG 4. The main goal of SDG 5 is to “achieve gender equality and empower all women and girls” (Manzano 2021). The United Nations points out that gender bias limits our human potential by denying half of the world’s population with proper opportunities. SDG 5 is critically important because without gender bias and prejudice, all nations and populations would benefit (The Global Goals, n.d.). Similar to SDG 4, the goal of gender equality has various targets to be reached. (5.1) End discrimination against women and girls, (5.2) End all violence against and exploitation of women and girls, (5.3) Eliminate forced marriages and genital mutilations, (5.4) Value unpaid care and promote shared domestic responsibilities, (5.5) Ensure full participation in leadership and decision-making, (5.6) Universal access to reproductive health and rights, (5.7) Equal rights to economic resources, property ownership and financial services, (5.8) Promote empowerment of women through technology, (5.9) Adopt and strengthen policies and enforceable legislation for gender equality (The Global Goals, n.d.). The year 2024 was a super election year and many communities and advocacy groups were hoping to see more women representation in politics. Though the elections provided ample opportunity to many women, the super election year actually did very little for gender parity (United Nations, n.d.). It is reported that as of January 2024, 27.2% of national parliament seats were held by women. This report provides little progress given only a 0.3 point increase in political representation from 2025. Though since 2015, representation of women in national parliament seats has increased by 4.9 points (United Nations, n.d.). Something very interesting yet frightening happened during this super election year in 2024. During this year, nearly 1 in 4 countries reported a “backlash” against women’s rights (UN Women 2025). This is concerning given that much of the progress made for gender equality before 2024 was lost. Many women also live near dangerous conflict regions, further intensifying pre-existing gender norms and roles. It is reported that in 2023, nearly 612 million women and girls lived within 50 kilometers from an active conflict zone (UN Women 2025). With this in mind, the United Nations have also proved that peace typically lasts longer when women’s voices are heard in the decision-making process (UN Women 2025).

Chapter 4 Conclusion The seventeen Sustainable Development Goals allow for conversation, advocacy, and action to take place in a productive way. Specifically, SDG 4 (quality education) and SDG 5 (gender equality) are deeply connected. Gender disparities and a lack of equality are embedded in the roots of quality education throughout the world. While boys have the privilege of going to school, boosting their likelihood of success, women and girls in many regions of the world are forced to stay at home (Charity:Water, n.d.). Not only are girls forced to stay at home and conduct domestic activities, but in many cases, girls will walk miles and miles to fetch water for their household (Charity:Water, n.d.). Keep in mind, this is all while their male counterparts are advancing their learning skills while being at school. Results from a lack of quality education typically impacts women and girls at a much higher rate than men and boys. Civilization has been dealing with these issues for all of human history yet right now, with the help of the Sustainable Development Goals, vast progress can be made. One data story is not enough to truly understand these complex issues which is why I will continue working on this project throughout the rest of the semester. Data Story 6 will go into more detail regarding these key issues (quality education and gender equality). My final data story will provide various graphing elements, plots, tables, and a discussion based analysis to further both my own and the viewers understanding of these problems. By focusing on SDG 4 and SDG 5, it is evident that both goals can reinforce one another, creating room for growth to occur. One of the leading drivers of gender inequality is a lack of equally accessible quality education for all children. What is holding women and girls back from receiving a quality education? How can the global community push for better quality education in all regions of the world? In my final project, data story 6, these difficult questions will be tackled and a clear analysis will hopefully come to the surface.
